data.title: Gold Technical Analysis: Bulls Defend $4700 Support; Is A Break Above $4750 On The Way?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has experienced a period of significant uncertainty this week as tensions in the Middle East remain on a knife's edge. As we head into the upcoming sessions, price action is consolidating around key structural levels, suggesting a breakout may be imminent. Will traders commit without a resolution in the Middle East?

Daily Chart: Long-Term Bullish Structure Remains Intact

Looking at the daily time frame, gold remains in a primary uptrend, supported by its position well above the 200-day Simple Moving Average (MA) currently sitting at $4238. However, the recent price action shows a significant cooling off from the $5,400 peaks.

The $4,700 level has emerged as a crucial floor for the bulls. This level previously acted as resistance and has now flipped to support, reinforced by the 100-day MA (blue line) which is currently tracking just above $4735. The RSI is hovering near the 47 mark, indicating a neutral momentum phase, neither overbought nor oversold, giving the metal plenty of room to move in either direction without immediate exhaustion.

H4 Chart: Falling Wedge or Bearish Continuation?

Moving down to the 4-hour chart, the picture becomes more nuanced. Price is currently oscillating within a descending channel or a large "falling wedge" pattern.

While traditionally a bullish reversal pattern, the H4 chart shows gold struggling to reclaim the 100 and 200 MAs.

The immediate hurdle for bulls is the $4804 resistance zone. A sustained break above this level and the upper boundary of the descending channel would be the first major signal that the corrective phase is over and the broader uptrend is resuming.

H1 Chart: Intra-day Scenarios and Key Levels

The 1-hour chart provides a clearer view of the immediate battleground. We are seeing a tight consolidation between the $4700 support and the $4750 resistance area.

Bullish Scenario: For a bullish move to materialize in the upcoming sessions, buyers need to clear the immediate intraday resistance at $4750.

A break above the descending trendline on this time frame would open the door for a retest of $4772 (200 MA) and eventually the major psychological barrier at $4800. A "bull" signal on the RSI Divergence indicator suggests that downward momentum is fading, supporting a potential bounce.

Bearish Scenario: On the flip side, the bears remain in control of the short-term trend. If gold fails to break above the H1 descending trendline, a retest of the $4700 support is likely.

A decisive hourly close below $4,700 would be a significant technical blow, potentially triggering a sell-off toward the next structural support at $4601.

Gold (XAU/USD) One-Hour Chart, April 23, 2026

Key Levels to Watch

  • Resistance: $4750, $4804, $4899
  • Support: $4700, $4601, $4500

Gold is at a crossroads. The daily trend remains bullish, but the intraday charts show a market looking for a catalyst. Traders should watch the $4700-4750 range closely. A breakout on either side of this corridor will likely dictate the direction for the remainder of the week.

Original Post

data.title: Credo's AI Comeback Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Just a few months ago, Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO) looked like a casualty of the AI cycle.

After rallying 400% in 1 year, Credo stock lost around 50% of its value, following the narrative that copper connectivity was a thing of the past.

But that narrative was wrong on two accounts.

Firstly, because Credo’s products are still a very integral part of the current AI supply chain.

And secondly, because with the acquisition of DustPhotonics, Credo has repositioned itself from a single-solution provider into a full-stack platform with both electrical and optical offerings.

Now Credo is back to all-time highs in just a month, and I’d argue there’s more room to run here.

The Great Selloff: When Narrative Overtook Reality

The sell-off we saw in Credo earlier in the year can be traced back to one specific moment.

Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang mentioned his company was exploring CPOs (co-packaged optics), and the market did the rest.

A whole narrative was built around the idea that copper does not scale, and therefore optics is the future.

This, in spite of the fact that Credo was delivering triple-digit growth and robust profitability.

Since then, Jensen himself has confirmed that Nvidia is pursuing both copper and optics.

Meanwhile, Credo has also decided to pursue a dual strategy by buying DustPhotonics.

The Strategic Pivot

The recent DustPhotonics acquisition has marked a turning point, both for sentiment and the stock.

DustPhotonics uses Silicon Photonics to create Integrated Circuits. In other words, it uses light, or optics, to deliver a similar end product to Credo’s.

By bringing silicon photonics capabilities in-house, Credo effectively eliminated the core bear argument to the investment thesis.

Now, it’s important to understand that Credo already has exposure to politics, as well as many other areas in the AI space, but it's driven by its AEC revenues.

With the acquisition, the Optics division could grow to around half a billion in 2027, and that may just be the beginning.

Why the Market Re-Rated the Stock

Following the acquisition, sentiment shifted quickly.

Within a month, Credo was back to new highs.

Firstly, because Jensen Huang reiterated that copper was still important to AI.

Secondly, the new acquisition gives Credo exposure to a key sector.

And lastly, because Credo actually got a good deal on the acquisition. As pointed out by fellow SA contributor Hunting Alphas, Credo bought the company for 4.4x–6.9x EV/revenue, which seems quite attractive given comparable recent moves in the industry.

The same factors that drove the selloff began to reverse.

What’s Next For Credo?

Looking ahead, the opportunity for Credo remains attractive, and the investment case compelling.

AI clusters are scaling rapidly, both in size and complexity, and this means bandwidth requirements also increase.

Now more than ever, there is a need for efficient, reliable, and, more importantly, scalable interconnect solutions.

The AI supercycle, or whatever you want to call it, is in full swing, and Credo is a key player.

Copper remains and will remain relevant over the next few years at least.

AEC is expected to grow at a CAGR of 22.8% over the next 6 years.

Meanwhile, optics could be a significant expansion opportunity, and Credo can now benefit from this.

This market is likely to grow even faster, at 35.5% CAGR.

Valuation and Positioning

But after a 400% rally, is Credo overvalued?

By most metrics, yes, but what really matters here is PEG and forward PEG, and that’s where Credo, like many of these fast-growing AI companies, still looks cheap.

Credo is expected to deliver triple-digit earnings growth in 2026 and strong growth after that. If we look at 2027 P/E, it’s not cheap, but it becomes much more reasonable.

A company with this margin profile and growing EPS at 30% should command a P/E of 30, since this would balance out PEG at 1.

I’d say there’s at least some upside based purely on a conservative valuation, and probably more given momentum and narrative.

Risks

But we still have to acknowledge some of the key risks.

With any merger, a lot of growing pains can occur, and execution risk is now very real. Delays or integration challenges could impact both margins and timelines.

And while Credo is working to stay ahead of the market, the breakneck pace of today's technological innovation is hard for anyone to keep up with. Who knows what kind of technology will appear in the next 5 years?

And of course, like many semiconductor companies in the AI space, Credo relies heavily on a small number of hyperscaler customers.

Final Thoughts

All in all, Credo’s selloff last month was unjustified, and I think there’s still room for the company to run higher from here.

data.title: Kaltura: Disappointing Trends And Over-Reliance On M&A (Downgrade)
data.description:
Since the start of 2026, the entire software industry has been turned upside down by the threat of AI. Software stocks have gone from being the hottest, highest-multiple growth stocks in the post-COVID era to the deep value plays that seemingly no investor wants to touch.

Small- and mid-cap stocks have seen a disproportionate amount of the pain, and this has left Kaltura, Inc. (KLTR), a video content management platform, down ~20% since January and down ~40% over the past year. The question for investors now is, is Kaltura beyond saving?

I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on Kaltura in November, when the stock was trading closer to $1.80 per share. Needless to say, performance in the stock since then has been frustrating. While I think the overall SaaSpocalypse narrative is overblown, it does force us to take a fresh look at every software name. Unfortunately, most catalysts have moved in a negative direction for the company. Growth trends have worsened, and while the stock remains ultra-cheap to counterbalance its risks, it's now joined in the value pile by dozens of higher-quality, faster-growing software businesses. Kaltura is no longer a rare value gem, and with that in mind, I'm dropping my rating on the stock to "Neutral."

At current share prices, I see a more balanced bull and bear case for the company. On the bright side for Kaltura:

  • Video content continues to enjoy rising popularity. Kaltura's core focus on video content enjoys secular tailwinds. It's becoming clear, especially with the growing popularity of the likes of TikTok, that brands need to have a concise short-form video strategy to capture consumers' attention, and Kaltura's technology fills that need nicely.
  • Increasing efficiency. Even amid top-line softness, Kaltura has boosted its adjusted EBITDA margins, and it has ambitions to return to double-digit revenue growth and achieve a "Rule of 30" growth/profitability balance by FY28.

At the same time, however, plenty of risks are cropping up and becoming tough to ignore:

  • Tepid growth and persistent churn issues. The company is trending at near-zero growth rates, is losing ARR, and has suffered multiple successive quarters of net churn.
  • Media & telecom headwinds. A pullback in budgets from the company's media & telecom vertical, which is roughly one-third of Kaltura's revenue, is a major headwind.
  • Increasing reliance on M&A. The company just announced a $22 million acquisition of PathFactory, set to close in Q2 of this year, on the back of a ~$27 million purchase of eSelf. These M&A deals to me are an implicit suggestion that Kaltura doesn't have the product chops to sustain growth as the IT landscape shifts to AI.

While we can't argue with the fact that Kaltura remains cheap, it's now just one of many in a sea of bargain software stocks. At current share prices near $1.20, Kaltura trades at a market cap of $180.7 million. Netting off the $62.6 million of cash on Kaltura's latest balance sheet against $29.0 million of debt and $22.0 million of cash consideration due for PathFactory (which is included in Kaltura's forward guidance), the company's resulting enterprise value is $169.1 million.

For FY26, as shown below, Kaltura has guided to $182.7 million in midpoint revenue (just +1% y/y growth, including the contribution from both the PathFactory and eSelf deals) and $13.7 million in adjusted EBITDA, which indicates a 7% midpoint margin (down 4 points y/y, with the acquisitions expected to water down the company's profitability profile).

This positions Kaltura's valuation multiples at:

  • 0.9x EV/FY26 revenue.
  • 12.3x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.

To me, Kaltura has lost much of its appeal. Though it's true that even among penalty-box software stocks it's difficult to find a name trading at <1x revenue, Kaltura's loss of margin and its persistent churn make it difficult to bet on for the long haul. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Kaltura's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Kaltura's revenue in Q4 declined 0.2% y/y to $45.5 million, slightly beating Wall Street's expectations of $45.3 million. The company notes that it signed a number of large new deals in the quarter, including for the company's AI-powered modules. Per CEO Ron Yekutiel's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

New subscription bookings in the fourth quarter were at the highest level of 2025. We closed 2 7-digit and 15 6-digit new deals across industries, including technology, financial services, health care, manufacturing, education and media and telecom. We also closed 7 AI-related deals for Content Lab and Genie, reflecting continued customer interest in our automation and personalization capabilities. Gross retention in the fourth quarter was also stronger than in any previous quarter in 2025, and we concluded the year as expected with the highest EE&T gross retention level in 5 years.

But the key question for Kaltura is, can it really retain its revenue base? Other metrics show that it cannot, despite the company's assertion that gross retention in Q4 was strong. In Q4, we notice that the company's ARR lost ~$1 million sequentially to land at $168.2 million, which was also a 3% y/y decline.

Meanwhile, Kaltura's net revenue retention rates also remained low at 97%, indicating 3% net churn.

The biggest fear in the software sector right now is that elevated competition from AI, plus the threat of layoffs that are putting pressure on software company seat counts, will impact embedded software companies' ability to retain their top lines. Kaltura's losses may have little to do with AI, but the poor recent trends in retention certainly don't do any favors for the stock's sentiment when the market is losing confidence in software companies' ability to retain business.

For now, we note that the company's profitability profile is improving. Adjusted EBITDA in FY25 nearly tripled y/y to $18.6 million, or an 11% margin that improved 7 points y/y.

However, I'm growing more skeptical about this profitability on two fronts. First, nearly the entirety of that adjusted EBITDA was driven by $16.5 million in stock-based comp addbacks. At present market values near ~$181 million, that's nearly 10% of share-based dilution. Second, we note that the company is expecting margins to crater back down to 7% in FY26, driven by the dilutive impact of acquisitions.

Key Takeaways

With Kaltura guiding to both tepid revenue growth (+1% y/y despite two newly acquired companies contributing to next year's revenue) and margin compression, it's easy to see why the market has soured on this stock. An ultracheap valuation multiple keeps me from being overly bearish on the company (as Kaltura itself may be a viable, cheap M&A target), but this is no longer a stock to bet long on.

data.title: EWP: Expect A Slow Tourism Season Amid Surging Fuel Costs And Weak Consumer Sentiments
data.description:
Spain, long an economic laggard, has emerged as one of Europe’s growth engines in recent years, with tourism playing a key role. I covered the iShares MSCI Spain ETF (EWP) back in January, and since then EWP is up about 4.5%, edging out the S&P 500 (~4%) in that same time period. Unfortunately, however, I suspect that Spain will face headwinds this summer, as I believe vacationing will cool off amid surging fuel prices, rising costs of living, and weakening consumer sentiments. I believe this tourism cool-off will be broad, geographically speaking, although value-oriented countries and regions may take less of a hit. With that in mind, I am downgrading my rating for EWP to a hold.

EWP and Tourism

First, I should note that EWP doesn’t hold a large amount of hospitality or leisure stocks. Finance, utilities, and industrials actually make up most of the portfolio. You might wonder then why I am focusing on tourism while analyzing EWP. My reasoning breaks down to some key points:

  • Roughly 13% of employment in Spain is related to tourism. If tourism cools and unemployment rises, consumption overall will cool, and people may fall behind on loans, credit cards, etc., thus hurting the finance sector.
  • Roughly 20% of businesses in Spain are tied to tourism (ibid.). Business loans, business consumption, utility usage, etc., could all decline substantially.
  • Construction and government spending on infrastructure could decline, dragging down the economy and hitting firms like Ferrovial SE (FER).
  • Amadeus IT Group, S.A. (AMADF), makes up about 4% of EWP. While this is a technology firm, it focuses heavily on tourism. A global tourism slump would have an especially outsized impact on this company.

All in all, tourism accounts for about 13% of Spain’s GDP. If the tourism sector does take a hit this summer, it’ll have a broad impact on Spain’s economy, and that will all but inevitably show up in stock markets. As such, while EWP is not as heavily tied into tourism directly as one might expect, its fortunes will still to a considerable extent be tied to the performance of the tourism sector.

I would expect other tourism-heavy countries/economies to take similar hits. The more directly a country ETF is tied to tourism, the bigger the likely impact, but even ETFs with low direct exposure will likely still be dragged down.

Why Tourism Is Likely To Take A Big Hit This Summer

In 2025, tourism was one of the key growth engines not just in Spain but for all of Europe. Record tourism numbers helped fuel strong economic growth. Monaco saw its GDP expand by more than 5%, while Malta grew by nearly 4%. Spain’s GDP was up 2.77%, while Greece grew by more than 2%. Germany, Austria, France, and Belgium, meanwhile, couldn’t crack even 1%. Also, keep in mind, tourism will generate demand across the region. A busy beachside restaurant might order napkins from Sweden and wine from France. A tourism slowdown, likewise, will impact everyone, which could result in a negative feedback loop forming across the EU.

Ultimately, vacationing is one of the most discretionary activities in any budget. As a result, when tough financial times crop up, like, say, an economic contraction, vacations are often among the first things to hit the chopping block. Even those folks who decide to go on vacation may opt to stay closer to home and spend less. Rather than enjoying fine dining, they may opt for cheap eats or preparing food in their hotel room, camper, or whatever.

While the global economy has shown resilience, consumer confidence is taking a big hit. I suspect this will result in consumers tightening their belts, especially on discretionary things like vacations. For many consumers, it may be better to skip an expensive vacation this year. If energy prices come down and the economy remains strong, they can go on a more expensive trip next year. In the EU, consumer confidence has been in free fall, down about 20% in April compared to long-term averages. Meanwhile, the United States saw one of the biggest hits to consumer sentiment on record, down 11% according to the University of Michigan.

One study found that during the 2007 financial crisis, countries highly dependent on tourism were more likely to suffer an economic slowdown/contraction and that the intensity of the economic disruption was typically more pronounced. If a recession were to occur, I’d expect Spain and Europe as a whole to have an especially bad time. Even without a recession, economic strain, such as high inflation, will likely have an outsized impact on tourism-dependent economies, and thus EWP. McKinsey recently reported that consumer spending intent was down considerably. This was for American consumers, but I expect that Europeans are in a similar boat.

Further, I suspect that long-haul tourism will take the biggest hit. After all, rising fuel prices will make long-distance travel especially expensive. This is bad news for Europe and Spain, as long-haul travelers are a big source of spending. Travel from the USA to Europe was up 5% in 2025 from a year prior. This year, growth could slow or even contract. American tourists are especially valuable as they tend to be big spenders, due in part to the fact that Americans have shorter vacation time. Euro Disney struggled when it first opened back in the 90s because Europeans, who have to plan for four weeks or more of vacation time, try to keep spending per day low. Americans, on the other hand, have less leisure time, and so many cram in more spending per day. Some outlets are reporting, by the way, that more Americans plan to stay in the USA this year.

Jet Fuel Is Especially Worrisome

Jet fuel prices, as shown above, have been spiking. Many European airlines are announcing that they’re cutting and consolidating flights. Experts are warning that Europe could actually run out of jet fuel in the months ahead. Jet fuel accounts for about 25 to 30% of an airline’s operating costs. Thus, surging fuel prices will have a very large impact. So far, long-haul tickets are up about €88, while in-Europe tickets are up about €29. Many experts expect price increases to begin accelerating if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.

EWP Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

Since I’ve taken an in-depth look at EWP’s portfolio in the past, I’m going to keep this brief and highlight some key changes. Crucially, as risks have mounted owing to Hormuz, inflation, and other factors, EWP has become a bit more expensive. The P/E ratio has risen from 11.86x in mid-October, 2025, to 12.42x, according to Morningstar. The Price/Sales ratio has increased even more to 1.51x, up from 1.16x.

Back in October, I also compared EWP to the iShares MSCI Germany ETF (EWG). Germany relies much less on tourism and focuses substantially more on industry. Industrials make up nearly 30% of EWG’s portfolio (compared to about 13.5% for EWP). While I expect the industrial sector will suffer pain too, I do think Germany may fare a bit better than Spain and southern Europe in general over the summer. Interestingly, EWG has maintained both its P/E at ~14.5x and P/S at ~1.15x, whereas EWP is ticking upward.

EWP is pretty generous as far as dividends go at 4.46%. That said, keep in mind that dividends are often sacrificed during economic slowdowns. If Spain does struggle with slow growth, the dividend yield could quickly shrink.

Why I Could Be Wrong

If and when the crisis in the Middle East is resolved, I expect EWP and other ETFs covering tourism-reliant countries in the EU to enjoy a nice pop. If a resolution is found quickly and it looks like it will be extensive and durable, then EWP could enjoy sustained growth. That said, the impact of the conflict will likely continue to push costs upward even with a quick resolution. If EWP does pop after the Hormuz issue is resolved, I would consider a “sell” rating.

Beyond that, consumers have proven to be surprisingly resilient in recent quarters despite sustained inflation pressure. Perhaps they will continue to show strength, and they may shrug off rising airfare and other increasing costs. If so, vacations could remain at the top of spending priorities for many.

Key Takeaway: Spain and Thus EWP May Suffer Some Pain

I think there’s a high chance that tourism will take a hit this summer, failing to match up to what were, before the Hormuz crisis, high expectations. As such, I believe that EWP will struggle this summer and is likely to underperform compared to the S&P 500. As for longer-term prospects, Spain has a lot to offer, which is why I am not going with a sell rating.

data.title: Markets Are Stuck In The Waiting For U.S.-Iran Talks
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Those who were looking for action this week barely got served.

A single session of volatility occurred on Tuesday, when rumors of failing talks between the US and Iran could lead to a re-starting of the war as the ceasefire deadline came close.

But it wouldn't be a normal Tuesday without a classic Trump TACO.

While both parties were preparing to resume the conflict, with 4 hours to the end of the ceasefire, the President extended the ceasefire without any clear deadline and allowed priorly anxious markets to explode higher in overnight futures trading.

Since, however, markets have been in an absolute deadlock, with rare movements across metals, stocks, currencies and cryptos (which couldn't find the momentum to sustain their past session's move).

The latest narratives are pointing to infighting between the Revolutionary Guards and a more progressive governmental party in order to bring diplomats to the table.

On the US side, they really are eager to push for a peace deal, so except if they fail to align a team for the talks, the process should still be towards peace (which is why stocks are staying at relative highs).

Check out this passive-aggressive post by the President!

Nobody knows what the Islamic regime has in mind, so except for gamblers, participants will have to remain patient and lean on current pricing to prepare for breakout plays when some news drop.

Let's check out a few charts to get an idea of where to look.

Stock markets are stuck at all-time highs

Stock markets haven't been able to generate much traction in recent sessions, and the flows should remain the same as long as no concrete news emerges for the peace in the Middle East.

Nasdaq keeps grinding higher

Tech investors have been fearless! Nasdaq is up close to 18% from its war lows. A shocking rise that left most investors scratching their heads. Watch out for a slowdown in momentum, showing small bearish divergence.

Monday's open will be essential, with more news expected by that time.

The Dow Jones could actually reflect the positive but cautious stance from investors, while the rech-heavy index gets pulled higher by pre-earnings inflows into Mag 7s.

Check out intraday levels for stock markets right here.

Silver rejects the $80 level

Metals are really struggling to find traction in recent action with a fake-out above $80 and finding pressure from a still-not-advancing Middle East situation.

Bears are taking the upper hand for now, having broken the bull channel from the ceasefire ecstasy. Watch for a break of $74 for bearish acceleration.

Traders will have to be cautious for continued profit-taking, but overall, it seems that the action is long-term range-bound ($65-85) for now.

Cryptos can't hold their move higher

Traders will have to see if they manage to retain a bid even if the narrative worsens.

The US Dollar rallies, but keeps FX markets in a deadlock

The US dollar has mean-reverted highs after a test of its mid-range 98.00 support.

Bouncing to 2-week highs today, the move seems contained by the narrative and forming a downward channel. This pattern could be fragile, particularly if the war were to resume in the absence of talks.

As always, make sure to follow talks around US-Iran negotiations.

Safe trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Lam Research: The AI Upcycle Is Real, But The Price Is Too Expensive
data.description:
Introduction

Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) has appreciated by over 93% since I last covered it in October and gave it a Hold rating. Since I last reviewed it, Lam has been able to navigate both a complex geopolitical trade environment as well as an expansion of its served addressable market. With the latest earnings in, I’ll dive into why Lam might need a stock re-rating.

Current Dynamics

Lam just released its fiscal Q3, which came out to a double beat as revenue stood at $5.84B, a slight beat of $80MM, but an impressive 23.7% Y/Y growth rate. On the bottom line, Lam posted a non-GAAP EPS of $1.47, which was a strong beat of 11 cents. The quarter was basically a record for Lam in terms of both top and bottom line, which raised the industry-wide outlook for the remainder of the year. It beat the firm’s own guidance in terms of revenue, and this was largely thanks to the customer support business group and advanced packaging applications.

Diving deeper, it reflects the intense technological shifts occurring in the semiconductor market as the foundry applications remained the dominant driver of systems revenue at 54%. Though this was a Q/Q decline from 59%, this was mainly linked to an explosion of memory-related investments. DRAM revenue reached an all-time high as well, accounting for 27% of systems revenue, up from the previous quarter at 23%. This is largely attributed to the transition to 1c node devices and the high capital intensity of high bandwidth memory ("HBM") production.

On the NAND side, it still represents the smaller portion of the revenue mix at 12%, though it has shown sequential improvement. Management has stated that the NAND market is on the verge of a major upgrade cycle, with $40B in conversion spending anticipated as customers transition to architectures exceeding 200 layers. This demand is effectively a direct result of AI data center requirements for high-speed and high-capacity enterprise SSDs.

Looking ahead, one of the main elements of the latest earnings release was the formal increase in the 2026 wafer fabrication equipment outlook. Lam initially forecasted a WFE spend of around $135B for the year, but this was revised upwards to $140B with an apparent bias to the upside. This is highly significant as it reflects the demand for AI compute and storage, which is effectively outstripping previous supply chain capacity. It also indicates that the constraints related to clean room availability and fab construction are being mitigated more rapidly than anticipated. Lam is also expecting its own SAM to expand as a percentage of WFE, as management is anticipating SAM to exceed the mid-30% range, toward maybe 40%. This would be thanks to the transition toward more complex 3D architectures such as GAA logic and 1c DRAM, requiring a higher intensity of the specific deposition and tech processes in which Lam is a market leader.

This 3D architecture is one of the best growth avenues for the firm, as Lam basically has the technological leadership in this, especially at critical device inflections. Since the end of 2025, Lam has effectively successfully commercialized several platforms that address the atomic-scale challenges of the AI era. The shift toward the Akara platform represents one of the biggest changes in the firm’s etch portfolio in two decades. This was designed specifically for the extreme precision required for GAA transistors and 3D memory architectures.

To meet all of this accelerating demand environment, Lam has also implemented a multifaceted operational strategy focused on global manufacturing. For example, the firm has been opening and ramping up manufacturing facilities in Malaysia, which has been a critical factor in the company’s recent margin expansion. The first plant has drastically improved factory efficiencies, contributing to the structural gross margin floor above 49%. The second facility is set to ramp up in H2 of the year, which will coincide with rising customer visibility and long-term fab construction activity, thus ensuring that Lam can manage the longer lead times that are in effect in the industry.

Valuation

Lam was not cheap when I first rated it, and the valuation multiples have continued to expand at a P/E GAAP of 54.5, at the higher end of the firm’s peers such as Applied Materials (AMAT) at 41.33 and ASML (ASML) at 49.13. The same trend could be seen at an EV/EBITDA that is now standing at 45.02, also the highest among its peers. The rise in stock price has also depreciated by a wide margin the firm’s FCF yield that is standing at 1.8%, which is vastly below the firm’s historical average. I also did a scenario-based analysis that would imply that the price is just right at this current juncture, with a weighted price target of $268.05.

The fastest and biggest growth vector is effectively advanced packaging, which is set to grow over 50% in 2026 thanks to the need for 2.5D and 3D architectures to integrate logic accelerators with HBM. Lam is holding near-total market ownership in the critical drill-and-fill process steps, which are essential for HBM production. The NAND upgrade cycle is also a major conversion cycle that is currently underway and should bring $40B to be pulled forward before the end of 2027, which should continue to increase revenue for the firm in the near future.

Risks

The main headwind remains geopolitical tensions, mainly regarding China. For example, the 50% affiliate rule, which has been recently adopted, is expected to impact Lam’s 2026 revenue by around $600MM, as it restricts sales to Chinese entities with specific ownership structures. The MATCH Act would further restrict the sale and servicing of chokepoint tools such as cryogenic etch systems to Chinese facilities.

The demand for AI equipment is extremely high, but the pace of growth is physically constrained by the availability of clean room space at customer sites. If fab construction in regions such as the U.S. or Europe experiences delays, the current 2026 WFE forecast of $140B could act as a ceiling rather than a floor for revenue.

Conclusion

Lam has a lot going for it, that’s for sure, but in essence, I believe that the firm’s valuation has gotten ahead of itself, as it seems rightly priced right now. For long-term holders, I would just hold the position, but for new buyers, I believe that the risk-reward is rather neutral, as semi equipment has historically mean-reverted, as highlighted by Lam’s revenue contraction back in 2023. In that sense, I rate Lam Research as a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ServiceNow Q1: It Takes Time To Re-Rate Great
data.description:
ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) stock is selling off as I write this in the premarket on 23 April, and since my last analysis the equity is down -17%. I still own NOW at 2.5% of my portfolio's NAV, and I am considering adding more at this lower price. That said, I do not feel any particular call to add here, given I am already heavily in the software trade in other arguably higher-moat stocks.

The company just posted Q1 2026 results, and by all measures they were reasonably good. I would say there was little cause for concern, but given somewhat of a still-afraid market re software, it will latch onto anything in order to build its downside case further even amid resilient fundamentals. I think in H2 2026 NOW (and other software stocks further) will really start re-rating higher, so I would view this sell-off as a further strong accumulation window. Counter to popular opinion, when the equity goes down, my appetite to buy goes up; though that strategy only works when operational catalysts or sentiment-driven undervaluations are objectively in place.

Q1 2026 Earnings

The quarter itself was not bad, I'd go as far as to say it was good. Subscription revenue rose to 22% year-over-year. cRPO rose by 22.5%. RPO was up by 25%. But the market essentially sold off the quality of the raise, because while the full-year subscription revenue guide rose $205M at the midpoint, 125 bps of full-year growth is now coming from the Armis acquisition, and management indicated that without Armis the full-year guide was held.

We're looking at short-term margin headwinds here (full-year non-GAAP operating margin to 31.5% from the prior 32%, and full-year free cash flow margin to 35% from 36%). That's the basic math behind the sell-off, but this is incredibly short-term and misses the fact that the Armis acquisition will only dilute profitability temporarily. In the medium term, the Armis acquisition is important for ServiceNow because it signals a move toward a full-stack, AI-native security and workflow platform. This expands NOW to be a leader in cyber exposure management and will ring important to anyone following Anthropic's (ANTHRO) recent AI security-breach capabilities.

And let's be honest here, NOW's core franchise is still elite. There are 16 deals with over $5M in Net New Annual Contract Value, five with over $10M, and 630 customers above $5M Annual Contract Value, and 97% renewal. Moreover, 17 of the top 20 deals have over seven products, and new-logo Annual Contract Value is up by over 50%. This is the data that really counts. It makes it look evidently like NOW is gaining workflow density and wallet share and not defending a fading legacy seat model.

Regarding the point from the bears that "AI kills SaaS," monetization is shifting toward usage, not disappearing. In an expanding long-term agentic AI TAM, that's likely to get the business acting as a toll on tokens before long. That's a bullish long-term framework to consider, so we've got deep value here in NOW as a fundamental operating business. It's hard not to double down as I am researching this, but I'm refraining as I feel my portfolio architecture is already pristine as-is.

NOW's 2026 AI commitments were raised to $1.5B from $1B, and Now Assist customers over $1M Annual Contract Value grew by over 130%; 50% of net new business already comes from non-seat pricing.

2027 Inflection & Valuation

Management has explicitly mentioned that the operating margin and free cash flow margin should expand and normalize in Fiscal 2027 versus 2026 integration headwinds. That makes the first half of this year arguably an ideal entry window before sentiment begins to expand again in H2 2026. The stock will re-rate on evidence before actual results materialize. I think H2 2026 will start to show signs of organic acceleration, cross-sell from Armis, and continued scaling of AI monetization. Sentiment can definitely and likely will improve before 2027.

In the base case I expect a grind before a durable re-rating, but this is likely exceptional value and I wouldn't overthink an entry here. The ultra-alpha thesis just requires patience. I think about 5% of NAV is good for NOW stock, but I own it at 2.5% due to heavy software-value exposure already. On a supplemental note, NOW repurchased 20.1M shares in Q1 and still has about $4.2B authorized, which means management is managing dilution carefully.

In the following chart you can see that NOW is trading at about $90 in the premarket as I write this. That's really remarkably cheap and signals a price well below the 200-week MA with an oversold 14-week RSI. The following chart is the daily-intervals chart, which shows the stock in a downtrend but from a fundamental perspective with the 2027 inflection, we are clearly at or near the base here and patience is all that is required. The entry point is very good.

If the stock re-rates to a forward P/E of 40 by April 2027 with normalized EPS of $5.50 in April 2028, then, the stock price will be $220 in about 12 months. That indicates over 100% upside in the base case. The company's five-year average forward non-GAAP P/E ratio is 63.8, and forward long-term EPS growth is only -25% lower than the five-year average, so a re-rate from the present P/E of 25 to 40 appears conservative and reasonable to me.

Critical Risks to Consider

The biggest risk with the investment case is a prolonged "trust discount," as NOW is leaning into longer sales cycles, more pricing pressure, and less predictability. At the current entry point, slower expansion inside the base can hit the multiple before it hits revenue, especially important as the company's headline renewal rate is Annual-Contract-Value-based, not customer-count based.

Looking at my 2027-normalization thesis, which was basically outlined by management so doesn't take a genius to time, execution is the obvious swing factor. One specific aspect to watch out for is that if cross-sell lands later than hoped from recent acquisitions, then the current dip may be a pause before another reset, not the bottom yet. Even so, downside would be quite muted bar catastrophe, because deep value is already established in the equity price.

Then we have the fact that pricing-model changes may not satisfy customers. If usage scales faster than monetization, old SaaS multiples might not come back. That said, it's a small risk to take for such a cheap stock, and the upside-downside asymmetry is skewed positively. This nuanced issue here is not a loss of relevance, but a loss of software economics and margins. It's too early to tell how this plays out, but it needs to be on management's radar (the margin "moat").

Conclusion: Buy

I'm staying bullish on ServiceNow stock because I see a durable path forward here to strong execution and an equity re-rating in H2 2026. That said, as I'm already sufficiently positioned in the software-correlated return vector, I am not increasing at this price. My average cost basis is about $100 per share for NOW stock, and that is good enough value to me and could still net me a 100% return or more in 18 months.

The risks are real here, but that's often the case with pre-inflection deep value. The question is whether management has what it takes to execute well, and I think they do. There's little doubt in my mind that this investment will bear the fruit I want in time, but it's not a company I want to be overweight on for my own personal preference reasons. That decision comes down to portfolio architecture for me, but who knows maybe NOW at 5% of NAV for you is the right deal. The price is definitely better than right.

data.title: TeraWulf's AI/HPC Pivot: High-Conviction Secular Growth Play
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Brock Fassnacht as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Company Overview

TeraWulf Inc. (WULF), up until the end of 2024, was a bitcoin mining company: utilizing low-cost power at their Lake Mariner site in upstate New York. At the end of 2024, they have pivoted to being a colocation provider of AI compute. i.e., they handle the entire data center build-out from acquiring the land, getting access to grid-connected power, electrical infrastructure, and permitting for the build, and then building the data center. The only thing they don't provide is the most expensive part of a data center: the hardware (servers and GPUs). Once built, they are basically a landlord to a hyperscaler, like Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), for 10+ years and have very little overhead costs, resulting in 80-85% EBITDA margins.

Thesis

Terawulf's potential growth story as an AI data center developer is significantly discounted to where the stock trades at today. In 2025, TeraWulf signed a marquee deal with Fluidstack backstopped by Google at their Lake Mariner, New York site that massively rerated their stock. In many investors eyes, including my own, this unicorn asset that they had from their bitcoin mining days had now been monetized, and the future growth story was not obvious. However, management had been steadily sourcing additional power assets over the past several years—an effort that has recently become both visible and materially significant.

Late in 2025, during their Q3 earnings call, management hinted at the potential closing of multiple new sites. These sites turned out to be much more significant than I expected. On February 1st, 2026, they announced an addition of two new sites: one in Hawesville, Kentucky (480 MW of already available power), and another in Morgantown, Maryland (scalable to 1 GW). These sites add well over 1 GW of potential capacity and, importantly, a growth runway through the end of the decade. CEO Paul Prager has been pretty vocal over the last few weeks that their Kentucky site will be contracted by the end of Q2.

Furthermore, on March 15th they entered into a $500 million delayed-draw senior secured bridge facility with Morgan Stanley to finance the construction and development of this asset.

Sector Comps and TeraWulf's Advantage

The Bitcoin Miner to AI HPC data center transition was one of the most lucrative investment themes of 2025. Many may look at the charts and say, "I missed it" or that it was a quick hype cycle. And that very well may be true for some companies. But there are a few reasons why TeraWulf continues to be my favorite despite many companies pursuing a similar goal.

GPU Risk Avoidance

TeraWulf from the beginning has been against taking GPU risk. This may or may not be the optimal approach in the long run. If GPUs continue to see long useful life and rental rates continue to go up, companies doing this, like Iren Limited (IREN), CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), and Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), probably outperform. But, in the near term, funding GPU purchases seems increasingly difficult, especially at competitive rates. Raising capital at attractive rates is a key enabler for this business to work. TeraWulf raised over $3 billion at a 7.75% rate at the end of last year for their contract with FS/Google, and bonds are now trading well above par at around a 6% yield, signaling strong investor confidence.

Portfolio Diversity

As shown in the first image, TeraWulf operates across four distinct power grids. This geographic diversification meaningfully reduces long-term regulatory and grid-specific risk. In contrast, many peers, particularly in bitcoin mining, are heavily concentrated in ERCOT, the electrical grid in Texas, which is currently facing a significant interconnection backlog.

If a fund is allocating across multiple companies in the sector, I would guess they would prefer to allocate to TeraWulf, which has a diverse portfolio of assets, versus companies like Cipher Digital, Inc. (CIFR), Riot Platforms, Inc. (RIOT), Iren, or Core Scientific, Inc. (CORZ), who all have a large presence in ERCOT. Within each grid, there is their own set of operators, regulators, and political pressure, which has recently become controversial. Texas (ERCOT) has historically had abundant energy access and a pro-business regulatory environment, which makes it in very high demand (hence the significant backlog). TeraWulf's data centers in various regions around the US are also attractive from a hyperscaler's perspective, as all of them already have a presence in Texas.

Customer Quality

Management has consistently focused on securing high-quality counterparties. There are multiple companies in this sector that have exposure to CoreWeave, where counterparty risk can translate into a higher cost of capital. TeraWulf’s approach should support more stable cash flows and improved financing terms over time.

Management Background

The management team brings deep experience in power markets, where much of the management team has owned and operated power plants over their careers. This is increasingly relevant as hyperscalers face political and regulatory pressure to co-locate or directly procure power generation alongside data center capacity.

Brown Field Site Development

All the major sites that TeraWulf is developing are brown field sites meaning that there is already a significant amount of electrical infrastructure on the site. They incorporate this into the design of the data center, and it results in a lower overall build cost for the data center. This means lower CapEx, lower interest expense, and just more value flowing back to the equity. Because of management's background, they are experienced in navigating this process and have proven that they can do it at their flagship Lake Mariner site in New York.

For all of these reasons, I believe TeraWulf should be trading noticeably higher than its peers, who are Cipher, Applied Digital Corporation (APLD), Core Scientific, Hut 8 Corp. (HUT), Galaxy Digital Inc. (GLXY), and Riot Platforms. However, this has really not been the case, as all of them have traded together in the $5-8 billion range. Since the start of this year, the continued execution of WULF has started to show some stock outperformance vs. peers, and I expect this to continue as the year goes on.

Financials

When looking at the financials, it is a little ugly. Only over the next few quarters will the financials start to reflect the financial health that TeraWulf is in. Right now it still reflects the legacy bitcoin mining business, which will be decommissioned over the next year or two.

There are multiple convertibles and project debt being issued to fund this capital-intensive pivot. But, as long as the data center buildings get completed on time throughout this year (which, given the debt market, company updates, and satellite imagery, seems highly likely at this point), the company will have >$600 million EBITDA in 2027, and that is contracted out for 10+ years backed by Core42 and Google. This is more than enough to cover the ~$350 million of annual interest expense from the debt issued below.

The company also has over $3 billion in cash as of end of Q4 2025. Some of which is reserved for the continued build out to fulfill the Fluidstack/Google lease. They also raised a convertible note amounting to $1 billion with 0% coupon at the end of October 2025. According to the CFO, this fortifies the balance sheet, and they will be able to weather any macro storm. In the near term they should not need to raise a significant amount of equity (or any at all). Only project debt on the back of new contracts.

Valuation

Despite these material improvements, the macro backdrop of AI disruption and Iran War uncertainty has kept a lid on this volatile stock. Currently at ~$15 a share, we are trading at about 18X contracted EV/EBITDA (contracted EBITDA is roughly equivalent to 2027 EBITDA, barring no delays). On the surface, this might seem like a reasonable multiple to give an early-stage data center company when established REIT comps like Equinix and Digital Realty are trading at 25x.

This seems less reasonable when considering (1) management has clearly signaled that the Kentucky Site will be leased shortly by another hyperscale quality tenant. This will bring them over $1 billion in contracted EBITDA and have them currently trading at ~12-13X EBITDA. (2) Their contracts are 10-15+ year contracts with some of the highest credit quality customers in the world (Google and Saudi-backed Core42). (3) They have a proven model and have a further ~1.4 GW to build after Kentucky. Having 2 GW built by 2030 could result in $2.5B+ of EBITDA (assuming a conservative $1.3 million EBITDA per critical MW).

My current fair value is $30, reflecting a 21x EV/EBITDA multiple on the current contracts with Core 42 and FS/Google along with the expected lease of full Kentucky site. The share count below reflects all convertible bonds turning to equity and an additional 15 million shares being added to fund the next buildout, which may end up being conservative.

Upside to this value could come from better deals (revenue per MW) than previous deals. Along with material developments from other sites, most notably their Maryland 1 GW site. Further de-risking events will happen throughout the year as CB3-5 becomes fully built and operational, a product of their Fluidstack/Google contracts. Also, moving away from bitcoin mining entirely could be a positive catalyst, as many institutions are still hesitant to own any bitcoin-related exposure.

Downside

Next year will be a $600 million EBITDA year for TeraWulf, which, with a 20 EV/EBITDA multiple (below any M&A deal valuations below), would be a $12 billion EV. Assuming they sign the Kentucky lease, that will get them to $1 billion in run rate EBITDA. Add a 20x multiple to $20 billion EV.

$12 billion to $20 billion EV and about $6-$9 billion in debt equates to about $11 to $20 a share as the downside. Not too bad for a high-growth company.

Risks

Execution risk: Data centers aren't delivered on time, and contracts fall apart. There has been no evidence of this so far, and I closely monitor how TeraWulf's bonds are trading, which to me is the best gauge for how this is going.

Financing risks: Lending markets dry up, and they do not have access to capital, which is critical for this capital-intensive business.

Technology developments that make AI compute dramatically less power intensive or make energy access more abundant.

There have been some, I'll say, questionable actions that appear to be very beneficial to management, specifically CEO Paul Prager, and may be questionably beneficial to shareholders, which were addressed in an open letter.

CEO Paul Prager's own company, Beowulf, was acquired by TeraWulf last year in order to vertically integrate the company. Beowulf operated multiple power assets, mostly owned by TeraWulf.

TeraWulf also acquired their second site in New York, Lake Cayuga, which was owned by a holding company that CEO Paul Prager was the owner of.

Catalyst

The main catalyst that management keeps hinting at is a deal at their Kentucky site. This build will occur over the next year and a half and be completed at the end of 2027. Besides just the deal being announced, there are some details to watch for that may bring more upside. (1) EBITDA per Critical IT MW per year. The most recent deal has been about $1.3 million. Given shortage of compute in the market, there could be upside. I don't think management would agree to a deal that is worse at this point. (2) Tenet quality. The previous deal was with Fluidstack, backed by Google. Ideally, we would lease directly to a hyperscaler and one other than Google to diversify customer streams. Look for Microsoft, Meta, or Amazon.

The next catalyst will be completion of their current construction builds (CB-2B is now complete. Full Core42 capacity online). This means revenue and cash flow will start to flow into the PNL. Or any further developments from other sites in their pipeline (see below).

data.title: Incyte Q1 Preview: The Growth Story Remains Intact
data.description:
Reassessing My Previous "Buy" Call On INCY

When I first looked at Incyte Corporation (INCY) stock in mid-January 2026, the stock looked interesting as it started to consolidate a little after a strong run-up throughout 2025. The growth story looked strong at the time—the firm's profitable biotech business doubled since 2021, and the non-Jakafi portfolio (Jakafi, ~60% of INCY's sales driver, is set to go through an LOE process in 2028) looked promising. Most things were looking good, so it is a bit frustrating to see the stock's price action following my bullish call—INCY has lost over 8% of its market capitalization since I posted, even though my undervaluation findings at the time pointed to a 33% upside potential.

Despite the sell-off, the core turnaround story hasn't really changed, as far as I can see. The non-Jakafi business line is still growing rapidly, and the firm holds a lot of cash on its balance sheet, which de-risks this turnaround to some extent. The undervaluation has become even wider, so I think INCY still deserves its "Buy" rating. In my opinion, 2026–2027 is very likely to be another strong period for the stock, similar to what we saw throughout 2025.

Why Do I Think So?

In its fiscal Q4 2025, INCY had a nice 28% YoY growth in revenues ($1.51 billion), beating the consensus estimate by over 11%, according to Seeking Alpha data. On the bottom line, however, we saw a miss of 6%, so it drove down the whole sentiment, and the stock closed the earnings trading session with a steep 9%+ correction:

The reason there was that EPS miss is in INCY's reinvestment back to its business—the company ramped up some parts of its OPEX to support an aggressive slate of late-stage clinical trials and prepare for four major product launches in the next 12 to 18 months (that's my understanding based on the management's commentaries).

For our late-stage pipeline, we anticipate FDA filing acceptance for povorcitinib in HS this quarter, and we plan to submit an sBLA for tafasitamab in first-line DLBCL in the first half of 2026. With those submissions underway, we expect the potential approval and launch of 4 products in late 2026 and early 2027. The emphasis in '26 shift to advancement across the portfolio as we expect 7 data readouts this year, including the positive tafasitamab data already shared in January and 14 pivotal trials underway across hematology, oncology and immunology by year-end. Together, this reflects a pipeline that is increasingly focused, more mature and positioned to translate scientific progress into meaningful impact and long-term value creation.

Source: INCY's Q4 earnings call

In my view, the impact on the bottom line was totally justified, and I believe these reinvestments are beneficial for the long run as they support the growth story beyond 2026.

Right now, the market is looking at INCY through the lens of its core product—Jakafi—that is going to lose its patent exclusivity in 2028. That's why we see a projected EPS drop of over 61% YoY in FY2029, according to Seeking Alpha:

The out-of-consensus view that I have is that Incyte will be able to offset that LOE impact almost entirely by its non-Jakafi sales in the next few years. The management has already laid out its plan of how they're going to achieve that. Between 2026 and 2027, they're going to launch:

  • Jakafi XR (in extended release), which is a once-daily formulation that improves patient adherence by 15-25%. This is likely to drive up the transition from the old variant and lock in hundreds of millions in protected revenue well beyond the 2028 patent cliff;
  • Opzelura in Moderate Atopic Dermatitis (in Europe) that is projected to hit the atopic dermatitis EU market in 2H 2026. Based on the management's calculations, it can add $200-300 million in additional sales in the next few years;
  • Monjuvi in 1L DLBCL that can get approval in early 2027. The prospects look good as the Phase 3 frontMIND data show a 25% improvement in progression-free survival when added to R-CHOP, so Incyte can get a solid market share from the TAM;
  • Povrocitinib in Hidradenitis Suppurativa, a novel oral JAK1 inhibitor. It might be launched in late 2026/early 2027, being the first oral treatment approved for moderate-to-severe HS.

The overall pipeline looks quite solid to me.

We just need more clarity from the management team on the upcoming calls on what's already been made and how quickly they're moving on the previously announced plans.

By the way, Incyte is going to report Q1 fiscal earnings on April 28, 2026, so let me preview what's coming.

We've got the following full-year financial guidance, but nothing from the management on the upcoming Q1 in particular.

The analysts are seeing Q1 2026 EPS of $1.37 and revenues of roughly $1.23 billion, based on Seeking Alpha data. Given Incyte's historical tendency to surpass revenue expectations—they have managed to beat the revenue consensus in 7 out of 8 cases over the past 2 years—I think we are very likely to see the same thing for the upcoming Q1. I'm not so sure about the bottom line beat, though, as INCY isn't that good regarding EPS beating.

Anyway, I believe the headline figures might cause short-term price action, but a few things will be critical this time. First, the management previously guided that the FDA would accept the NDA (new drug application in this case) for povorcitinib in Hidradenitis Suppurativa in Q1 2026, so it's very important to hear what the CEO has to say on this front. If he confirms the acceptance, the stock is pretty likely to finally reverse to the upside because this drug might prospectively bring in a $1 billion+ franchise across HS, vitiligo, and PN.

Second, the FDA was expected to make its final decision on Jakafi XR in mid-2026, so I assume that the analysts will be asking the executives about that as well.

Third, we know that Incyte launched Opzelura for pediatric atopic dermatitis in late 2025, quickly annualizing at $30 million, so it's very interesting now how this drug performed throughout Q1 26. I think there's no evidence suggesting that the volumes ramp stopped, and that's why Opzelura should mark another strong QoQ growth in sales this time.

Fourth, I'm expecting to get an update on their collaboration with the FDA and Catalent regarding the manufacturing inspection issues that halted the NSCLC label expansion—a quick resolution timeline here points to a bullish catalyst for the stock's repricing, in my view.

Fifth, we need to see the updates on the Phase 3 trials for INCB161734 (the KRAS G12D inhibitor) in pancreatic cancer, plus the 989 (mutCALR) program in essential thrombocythemia (was expected by mid-2026).

I generally think the commentary will be positive; even if INCY doesn't beat on the bottom line again, there's a strong chance that positive management sentiment will help offset the reaction to the headline figures.

Now, let's take a look at the stock's valuation. On a YTD basis, the forward P/E ratio dropped by 3-4%, which is okay in general, but when compared to the TTM ratio, INCY looks clearly undervalued to me. The forward P/E of 13.12x is 13%+ under the TTM figure, and it's also almost 26% below the industry's median figure (~17.71x, per Seeking Alpha).

So, assuming that INCY doesn't see any upside EPS revisions for FY2027, but its non-Jakafi portfolio keeps gaining traction, and the market decides to grant the stock a higher multiple like in 2023-2025, the repricing looks like the most obvious scenario. At just 15-17x FY2027 earnings, INCY's fair value per share arrives at $139.68, which is 44% above the current stock price.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The biggest risk to my bullish thesis on INCY is the firm's still-heavy dependence on Jakafi sales. Although the core non-Jakafi part shows promising results and growth rates, Jakafi remains the elephant in the room, and we can't really know for sure what comes for INCY when the patent cliff time comes in. If INCY fails to reposition itself and convert some of the current Jakafi patients to Jakafi XR, we might see multiple EPS misses ahead, and the YoY dynamics on the top line will likely disappoint us as well.

Assuming that by 2027, INCY's P/E ratio drops to 8-9x, like in Pfizer's (PFE) case at some point, and we see no consensus revisions to the upside as well, the resulting fair value per share would drop to only $74.2. The downside in this case would amount to 23-24%, so it's a clear risk to keep in mind.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risk, I believe INCY looks good on its current dip. The stock seems to have already absorbed the key execution risks, and we just need to see the confirmation from the management team that their previously stated growth plan keeps working the way we saw in 2025. I don't expect a double beating this time as a core base scenario, but I do think the guidance and projections are going to be bullish, and it will likely lead to a repricing to the upside. If so, my PT of $139.68/share might be closer than it seems.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: ServiceNow: This Drop Is A Gift
data.description:
ServiceNow (NOW) announced on Wednesday that it beat the high end of its guidance across a number of key metrics, which in turn allowed the agentic AI player to exceed analyst consensus expectations for the Q1 top line. Nonetheless, shares slumped ~15% after the Q1'26 earnings report, mainly because the SaaS enterprise submitted a lower-than-expected outlook for its subscription gross margin for FY 2026 and delayed deal closings in the Middle East weighed on investor sentiment.

However, the AI-powered platform reported strong operating and free cash flow margins, with ServiceNow especially benefiting from strong momentum in its Now Assist product -- the company’s generative AI suite. A key driver of growth for ServiceNow is the platform’s ability to grow especially quickly with enterprise customers that have a platform spend of more than $5.0M annually, which are backing ServiceNow's strong margins. I believe investors are overreacting to the company's solid earnings report card, and I would recommend that investors buy the drop in shares of ServiceNow.

Previous rating

I rated shares of ServiceNow a ‘Strong Buy’ in my coverage initiation in March of 2026 -- A Strong Bet On Agentic AI -- because of the company's enormous potential in the agentic AI market. ServiceNow is growing highly lucrative enterprise accounts at a rapid pace and as a result lifted its subscription revenue guidance, but it lowered its margin guidance slightly. I feel that the 0.5 PP decline in margin guidance should not have wiped out $15.0B of the company's market cap and I would recommend emerging here as a buyer.

ServiceNow met Q1 EPS expectations

The SaaS platform, which is focused on the mass roll-out of autonomous AI agents in the enterprise market reported better-than-expected revenue results for Q1'26: ServiceNow published non-GAAP earnings of $0.97 per-share, which met the market consensus exactly. The revenue figure came in at $3.8B, surpassing the analyst expectation by $24.2M.

Strong revenue performance, fairly resilient margins

ServiceNow reported record subscription revenues of $3.67B for the first fiscal quarter, showing 22% year-over-year growth and exceeding the platform’s previous quarter guidance of $3.655B at the high end. The platform's remaining performance obligations/RPO -- a major future revenue indicator -- soared 25% year-over-year to $27.7B. ServiceNow's results were driven chiefly by accelerating product uptake of its generative AI suite, with deals seeing 3 or more Now Assist products growing ~70% year-over-year.

ServiceNow's revenues consist mainly of high-quality subscription revenues from high-grade enterprise clients. The platform's subscription revenues grew 22% Y/Y (+1 PP Q/Q) in the first-quarter amid strong AI-driven growth in the enterprise segment. Since subscription revenues are recurring and made up 97% of consolidated revenues in Q1'26.

However, the company also said that because of the Iran war, some deals in the Middle East were delayed, which represented approximately a 75-basis-point headwind. Since deals are expected to be merely delayed, there is a chance for the SaaS company to benefit from an acceleration in its subscription revenue growth rate in the second-quarter.

As I mentioned in the introduction, a key driver for ServiceNow to boost its profitability, as well as its revenue backlog, is to sign contracts with enterprise customers that are spending at least $5 million annually on the firm's products and services. In the most recent quarter, ServiceNow added a total of 28 enterprise customers in its portfolio that spent more than $5 million annually, bringing the platform's total high-value customer count to 630.

Driven by this momentum at the top end of the enterprise market, ServiceNow raised its Now Assist annual contract value/ACV (which measures the total value of all contracts signed) by 50% to $1.5B.

This upgraded outlook reflects accelerating adoption for ServiceNow's generative AI suite, which grants customers the ability to easily deploy autonomous agents across the enterprise landscape. The raised AI revenue target also highlights that the company's AI strategy is working and that the SaaS enterprise is successfully converting its AI-driven product suite into tangible revenue backlog.

Strong enterprise client conversion is what backs up ServiceNow's margin profile - although the SaaS platform has seen a slight decrease in its gross margins, on a non-GAAP basis, in the first quarter. In Q1'26, ServiceNow's non-GAAP gross margin was ~79.5%, showing a 1 PP decline compared to the previous quarter, reflecting the integration of the Armis acquisition alongside strategic investments in generative AI infrastructure. ServiceNow announced the acquisition of Armis last year (it closed in Q2'26) in order to boost its AI-driven security and risk management capabilities.

Non-GAAP metrics Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Q1'26 Y/Y Growth
Gross Margin 82.0% 81.0% 81.0% 80.5% 79.5% -3%

Valuation considerations

ServiceNow raised its revenue guidance for FY 2026 to $15,735-15,775M (+$205M at the mid-point), which implies 22-25% year-over-year revenue growth. However, ServiceNow also slightly lowered its subscription gross margin outlook (non-GAAP) to 81.5% vs. 82.0% previously. This small margin downgrade as well as the impact of some delayed enterprise deals in the Middle East triggered a 15% share price correction on Thursday, which I believe is exaggerated.

ServiceNow is a leading enterprise-focused agentic AI platform that helps companies tap into the power of artificial intelligence and roll out autonomous AI agents in order to streamline workflows and improve corporate efficiency. As such, the SaaS enterprise is tapping into the desire for companies to invest more money into AI products and services, which is driving a massive TAM expansion (the extent of which I discussed in my last work).

ServiceNow is being projected to grow its EPS at an average of ~20% annually in each of the next three years, and due to the exaggerated market response to the Q1'26 report, investors can now get an even bigger discount to the historical P/E ratio: shares currently trade at 20.5X forward earnings, based off of FY 2027 earnings, which calculates to a 52% discount to the historical valuation factors. For comparison, Oracle (ORCL) and Microsoft (MSFT) are trading at 23.5X and 22.9X earnings.

In my last work on ServiceNow, I calculated a fair value of $201 per-share for the company, which represents about 134% upside potential from a current price of ~$86. This price target, in my opinion, could be achieved in the next 3 years, assuming that the agentic AI market continues to expand as rapidly as projected and that the company grows its lucrative top-end enterprise market presence.

Risks with ServiceNow

The biggest risk for ServiceNow is increasing competition in the enterprise market now that the utilization of artificial intelligence has effectively lowered the entry barrier for developers. Lower barriers of entry in the software tech market mean now even small start-ups have the opportunity to offer high-quality software and AI services directed at the enterprise segment.

Further, ServiceNow is doing very well right now at the top end of the enterprise market by adding a significant amount of new contracts, which is boosting average account values. A slowdown in the speed of enterprise net adds could have a negative margin impact on the firm’s operating income and free cash flows going forward... in which case I could be tempted to lower my stock rating for ServiceNow.

Final thoughts

ServiceNow is doing very well right now in the enterprise market, and the firm’s Q1’26 earnings report card provided sufficient proof that momentum is not slowing down in dramatic ways for the agentic AI player: the SaaS platform is growing its subscription top line and revenue backlog with the lever of artificial intelligence. The Middle East impact on the firm's subscription revenue growth was negligible and should not be a long-term concerns for investors.

ServiceNow has also had great success in growing the most lucrative accounts in its portfolio -- those associated with large enterprise customers that are spending at least $5.0M annually on the company’s products and services. Further, ServiceNow closed the Armis acquisition which is set to boost the firm’s security stack and AI-driven automation capabilities in the future. I like the investment setup and don't believe shares should have dropped as much as they did following the Q1'26 earnings report. In my opinion, investors with a desire to build a software-oriented tech portfolio with AI exposure should consider the 15% share price drop of ServiceNow as an engagement opportunity.
